title: The Caddy

jokes.text: You know, you throw a rock into a crowd, that's considered terrorism. But if you have a nice follow-through, you know, that's golf.
jokes.setup: Jerry notes that 600 people crowd around a hole and are surprised when a Titleist bounces off someone's head — comparing it to throwing a rock into a crowd.
jokes.punchline: The only difference between terrorism and golf is having a nice follow-through.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, highly quotable observational premise that reframes a mundane sport in a darkly absurd way. The terrorism/golf juxtaposition is genuinely surprising and economical — the word 'follow-through' doing double duty (literal golf term, metaphor for intent). Strong stand-up construction. Justifies 8+ because it's a legitimately memorable joke with an elegant subversive twist.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I don't understand about golf? Why is it okay to hit a golf ball into the crowd at a tournament, but if you threw a rock into the stands, you'd be arrested? It's the same thing! You're hitting people with projectiles. The only difference is one requires a follow-through.

jokes.text: I'm always impressed with the golf cameraman whose job it is to follow the golf ball when it's in the sky. It's a little white ball doing 100 miles an hour on a white background. 'I got it. I lost it. I got it. I lost it.' Why bother? Aim the camera at the sky. Aim it at the ground. Take a ball out of your pocket. Throw it down. Who's gonna know where the hell it came from?
jokes.setup: The absurd premise that a cameraman's job is to track an almost-invisible ball at high speed against a matching background.
jokes.punchline: The camera solution: just fake it — nobody can tell the difference anyway.
jokes.explanation: Good escalating logic — the observation is grounded, the solution (just fake it) is a satisfying absurdist leap. The 'I got it. I lost it.' repetition is performatively funny. Slightly longer than it needs to be, but earns its punchline. Solid stand-up bit.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You ever notice the cameramen at golf tournaments? They're following the ball the whole time. Got it, lost it. Got it, lost it. That's their whole job. Got it, lost it. Like they're playing some kind of game with the ball.

jokes.text: How did you lock your keys in your car? How? Because I'm an idiot.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks incredulously how George managed to lock his keys in the car.
jokes.punchline: George's completely matter-of-fact self-assessment: 'Because I'm an idiot.'
jokes.explanation: Very economical — Jerry's repeated 'How?' ratchets up the expectation that the answer will be elaborate, and George's flat self-deprecation undercuts it perfectly. Perfectly character-consistent for George. The simplicity is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, if nobody can follow the ball anyway, why don't we just fake the whole thing? We'll have the cameraman just point at nothing for two hours.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's genius. Nobody would know the difference.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: We could be in a studio somewhere, the players could be playing checkers, and the announcers are just making it up as they go along.

jokes.text: George explains his car has been sitting in the Yankee parking lot for three days because he's waiting for his Auto Club membership to 'kick in.'
jokes.setup: George didn't get a locksmith because the Auto Club offers free locksmith service — so he signed up instead.
jokes.punchline: He's just... waiting for the membership to kick in. The car has been abandoned in a Yankee Stadium parking lot for three days.
jokes.explanation: This is deeply George — the logic of saving money by signing up for a membership leads to absurdly greater inconvenience. The reveal that the car has been there three days lands perfectly. The joke is also the inciting premise for the entire B-plot, making it structurally important. Gets a rewatch bonus because its payoff compounds throughout the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, how could you leave your car at the gas station?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know. I just did.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You don't know? It's your car!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm not a details guy, Jerry. I never have been.

jokes.text: Kramer arrives dressed for golf in January — 'Calendar says winter, but the gods of spring are out.'
jokes.setup: Kramer shows up unexpectedly and announces he's going golfing.
jokes.punchline: His grandiose self-justification — 'the gods of spring' — for playing golf on a closed course in winter.
jokes.explanation: Kramer's mythologizing of his own mundane decisions is a well-established comedic mode for the character. 'Gods of spring' is a funny phrase for sneaking onto a closed golf course in winter. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Jerry, this Auto Club is incredible. Free locksmith service. I just need to wait for the membership to activate.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How long does that take?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A few days. Maybe a week.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And your car is locked the whole time?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, but it's worth it for the peace of mind.

jokes.text: Stan's advice has transformed my game. He's never wrong... He thinks eventually I'll have a shot at making it big on the Senior Tour. Oh, that's my dream, Jerry.
jokes.setup: Kramer raves about his caddy Stan as though Stan is an oracle.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's 'dream' is the Senior Tour — a tour for golfers over 50, implying Kramer believes he'll be good enough to turn pro only when he's elderly.
jokes.explanation: Kramer's delusion is perfectly calibrated — he's so committed to the Senior Tour dream without any self-awareness about what it implies. The joke builds on his grandiosity and the absurdity of treating a caddy like a life coach.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're playing golf?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, why not? Winter golf, it's beautiful. The frost on the fairway, it's like a blanket of diamonds. And the ball, it travels further in the cold air. It's pure.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're insane.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I'm a purist. Most people, they only play in the spring and summer. But the real golfer, he plays year-round. It's about the challenge, the elements.

jokes.text: Stan the Caddy advises on George's cotton pants: 'Here's what you do. You bring a lightweight jacket. That way, the sun comes out, you play the jacket off the sweater.'
jokes.setup: Stan is introduced as a golf-advice savant. He immediately pivots to giving George wardrobe advice.
jokes.punchline: Stan's layering strategy is delivered with the same authoritative gravity as a major golf tip — 'That's a good call, Stan.'
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the gap between the reverence ('Stan's advice has transformed my game') and the banality of the advice (wear layers). Kramer and George treating Stan's wardrobe tip with total solemnity is funny. Sets up the running gag of Stan as an oracle.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I sneak onto the course through the service entrances. They can't stop me.

jokes.text: Kramer and George both say 'Stan the Caddy' in identical reverent tones as Stan walks away.
jokes.setup: Stan has just given his jacket-layering advice and departed.
jokes.punchline: Both men stare after him and echo his name like it's a mantra — 'Stan the Caddy. Stan the Caddy.'
jokes.explanation: A concise, well-timed button on the Stan introduction. The synchronized reverence from two grown men over a caddy is the joke. Economy is excellent — two words do all the work.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Jerry, I got it. Stan's advice has completely transformed my game. I'm gonna go pro.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Go pro? Kramer, you can barely break 90.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's the old Kramer. This is the new Kramer. I'm a different man out there on the course.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You shot a 127 last week.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Jerry, I'm not listening to the negativity. I've already called three tournaments. I'm in.

jokes.text: Oh, great. It's the braless wonder.
jokes.setup: Elaine runs into someone on the street she clearly has history with.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's internal (or muttered) nickname for her rival is 'the braless wonder' — delivered with absolute contempt before a bright fake smile.
jokes.explanation: Three words that establish a character, a rivalry, a physical characteristic, and a comedic tone. 'Braless wonder' is perfectly coined — sarcastic, specific, and slightly absurd. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Your pants are too cold for golf.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Too cold? They're pants!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: They're thin pants, George. You need insulation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I have a system. I wear the pants, then I put on a jacket. That's how it works.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not a system, that's just getting dressed.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It IS a system. It's MY system. I've been perfecting it for years.

jokes.text: Who does she think she's kidding? Look at her, she's totally out of control.
jokes.setup: Elaine watches Sue Ellen walk away, seething with barely-contained judgment.
jokes.punchline: The histrionic, disproportionate outrage — 'out of control' — applied to someone simply not wearing a bra.
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — Elaine's outrage escalates nicely, but this line alone is a setup for what comes next rather than a standalone joke. Works in context.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown woman
dialogue.line: Is it cold in here, or did you just get excited?

jokes.text: Locking your keys in your car is the best career move you ever made.
jokes.setup: Jerry and George realize that George's abandoned car in the Yankee parking lot is making Steinbrenner think he's the hardest-working employee.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's summary verdict — accidental incompetence as genius career strategy.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — one sentence summarizes the entire comic situation with maximum irony. The observation that George's stupidity accidentally made him look like a workaholic is a beautifully constructed ironic payoff for the setup established three minutes earlier. Highly quotable, strong structure. Justifies 8+ craft.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I just can't make it to your party. I'm swamped at work.

dialogue.speaker: Sue Ellen
dialogue.line: Well, you're still getting me a gift, right?

jokes.text: She's your Lex Luthor.
jokes.setup: Elaine has laid out the long history of Sue Ellen stealing her boyfriend in high school.
jokes.punchline: Jerry succinctly mythologizes the rivalry with a superhero villain reference — 'She's your Lex Luthor.'
jokes.explanation: Four words that perfectly reframe an extended backstory. The Lex Luthor comparison is exactly right — a wealthy, glamorous nemesis who defeats the hero through sheer charisma rather than evil genius. It's Jerry at his most economical and the comparison is surprising, apt, and deeply quotable. Justifies 8+ on craft: maximum economy, strong structure, character-specific (only Jerry would frame it this way), and genuinely original comic framing.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: George, I'm giving you a promotion. You'll be vice president of the Eastern Division.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Vice president? That's wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: But you must promise me one thing - you keep this confidential. Not a word to anyone until I make the official announcement.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Of course, Mr. Wilhelm. My lips are sealed.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: Good. Because if this leaks out before I'm ready, there will be consequences.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You can count on me, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: You're fired, Costanza.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? Why?

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: I don't like the way you looked at me. Now get out of my office.

jokes.text: Elaine announces her revenge gift for Sue Ellen's birthday: '...a very traditional... a very supportive... brassiere.'
jokes.setup: Elaine says she's getting Sue Ellen a birthday present, building anticipation with dramatic pauses.
jokes.punchline: The gift is a bra — the most passive-aggressive gift imaginable, delivered with the gravity of a grand gesture.
jokes.explanation: The rhythm of the delivery is everything — 'a very traditional... a very supportive...' — the pacing mimics the unveiling of something profound or romantic, making 'brassiere' a perfect comedic deflation. It's also the inciting incident for the entire episode's chain reaction. Elaine's passive-aggression distilled to its purest form.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what you're telling me is that by doing nothing, by being completely inert, you've become successful at your job?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, that's actually the same management philosophy the Yankees have been using for the last fifteen years.

jokes.text: 'There's nothing subtle about that.' / 'No, no, she might just think it's a gift.'
jokes.setup: Jerry points out the obvious — gifting someone a bra is not subtle.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's completely earnest denial that the message is obvious.
jokes.explanation: Elaine's self-deception is funny — she either genuinely believes her own cover story or knows she's lying. Either reading is comedic. Jerry's 'there's nothing subtle about that' is the straight-man setup. Solid exchange.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm out there, Jerry, and I'm lovin' every minute of it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What happened?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I went back to my apartment, I got on the computer, I'm looking at old-school baseball movies. I'm thinking about the good times. I'm thinking about how life used to be. And I said to myself, 'Why did I ever stop doing this?' So I decided I'm gonna start doing it again.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean you're gonna start doing it again?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm gonna start approaching women the way I used to. I used to have a little system, it worked pretty well. I would call women up, I would say things that made them laugh. They seemed to like that.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what changed?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know. Life. Confidence. Stuff. But you know what? It all just came back to me. Because I did something today that I haven't done in a long time. I did it because I felt like it. And you know what? It worked.

jokes.text: Have I ever bought you a jockstrap as a gift?
jokes.setup: The guys are debating whether the bra gift reads as passive-aggressive or just a gift.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's analogy — imagining receiving a jockstrap as a gift — flips the perspective to make the aggression obvious.
jokes.explanation: Elegant analogy that immediately clarifies and escalates the comedy. The jockstrap parallel is perfectly chosen — specific enough to be funny, close enough in logic to make the point devastating. High economy — one sentence does everything.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: She's been wearing the same bra since high school!

jokes.text: George says he'll take off from work — they won't notice because his car is in the parking lot. Jerry: 'Is this a good idea, with you being on the verge of this big promotion?' George: 'My presence in that office can only hurt my chances.'
jokes.setup: George is about to get a huge promotion based entirely on the illusion that he works extremely hard.
jokes.punchline: George's self-aware but still delusional logic: his actual presence undermines the façade — so he must be absent to succeed.
jokes.explanation: This is quintessential George Costanza — a perfectly inverted logic where incompetence is the correct career strategy. 'My presence in that office can only hurt my chances' is an extremely quotable, highly specific-to-George line. Only George, who has built an entire career fraud on absence and appearance, could deliver this with a straight face. Justifies near-8 craft.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So this woman is basically your Lex Luthor.

jokes.text: My presence in that office can only hurt my chances.
jokes.setup: Jerry questions whether it's wise for George to skip work when he's on the verge of a big promotion.
jokes.punchline: George's inverted logic — that physically being at work would be counterproductive — delivered with complete confidence.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant George Costanza line. It perfectly encapsulates his worldview — that the normal rules of work and effort are inverted for him, and that his presence is a liability. The confidence with which he delivers this lunatic logic is everything. Could ONLY be George. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor; justifies the score.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A gift? If I gave a woman a bra, she'd think I was saying, 'Hey, I've noticed your breasts. Here, strap 'em in.'

jokes.text: Sue Ellen arrives at Elaine's office wearing the bra as a top — Elaine stares, horrified.
jokes.setup: Elaine smugly gave Sue Ellen a bra as a passive-aggressive 'gift,' expecting her to be shamed.
jokes.punchline: Sue Ellen wears it as a top in public, completely undaunted — the passive aggression has backfired spectacularly.
jokes.explanation: The best situational payoff in the episode. Elaine's entire revenge scheme is not just defeated but reversed — Sue Ellen has turned the weapon back on Elaine by simply having no shame. The visual of a woman walking around in just a bra as a fashion choice is perfectly absurd, and Elaine's silent horror is the punchline. This moment is what launches the entire plot (Kramer crashes, lawsuit, fashion campaign). Justifies 8+ craft: perfect structure, character integration (only Sue Ellen would do this, only Elaine would be destroyed by it), and earned through careful setup.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're not going to take any time off?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No. No time off. If I don't take time off, if I show up every single day, they have to promote me. It's like a catch-22. They have no choice.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not a catch-22.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, I know. But it's close.

jokes.text: Elaine, barely holding it together: 'Well, Sue Ellen, it's.... It's not a top. It's a bra.' Sue Ellen: 'Oh, I know. Thanks again.'
jokes.setup: Sue Ellen stops by to thank Elaine for the gift, wearing it as a top.
jokes.punchline: Sue Ellen completely acknowledges she knows it's a bra — and doesn't care at all. Elaine's correction achieves nothing.
jokes.explanation: Sue Ellen's 'Oh, I know. Thanks again.' is a perfectly devastating reply — it concedes Elaine's point while rendering it completely irrelevant. The cringe of Elaine having her passive aggression not just neutralized but cheerfully acknowledged is exquisite. The economy of the exchange is excellent. Justifies 8+: character-specific, perfectly structured, devastating in its simplicity.

dialogue.speaker: Sue Ellen
dialogue.line: Thank you so much for the lovely gift.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, that wasn't a gift. That was just something I didn't want anymore.

jokes.text: George calls Jerry from the road and asks him to go to the Bronx to take fliers off his car. Jerry: 'Last time you had me throwing bread up three floors to you. Now you want me to go to the Bronx...'
jokes.setup: George needs a favor — remove fliers from his car in the Yankee Stadium parking lot.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's exasperated callback to an apparently even more ridiculous prior favor (throwing bread three floors), escalating the absurdity of their friendship.
jokes.explanation: The 'throwing bread up three floors' is a perfectly Seinfeld-esque offscreen absurdist aside — it implies a whole other ridiculous episode of George-Jerry codependency. 'Where does it end?' as a rhetorical question from Jerry is also funny because we know it never ends. Good escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's not a bra, it's a... it's a... bra

dialogue.speaker: Sue Ellen
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know what a bra is.

jokes.text: Jerry agrees to take fliers off George's car, then immediately recruits Kramer: 'Hey, what are you up to? Nothing. You wanna go to the Bronx and see if there's fliers on George's car? Sure.' Jerry: 'I could have said just about anything.'
jokes.setup: Jerry, having reluctantly agreed to the favor, immediately tries to offload it onto Kramer.
jokes.punchline: Kramer agrees instantly without hesitation, prompting Jerry's observation that Kramer would have said yes to literally anything.
jokes.explanation: Jerry's line 'I could have said just about anything' perfectly captures Kramer's character — his impulsive, uncritical willingness to participate in any scheme. It's also a meta-joke about how these plots work on Seinfeld. High character integration for Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You want me to drive six hours total to remove some fliers?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, what do you want me to do? I can't drive six hours.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I have to do it?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: If you don't, I'm never speaking to you again.

jokes.text: Jerry and Kramer arrive at the parking lot to clean up George's messy car — then realize: 'Oh, the keys are locked inside.'
jokes.setup: The whole reason the car is in the parking lot is that George locked his keys inside.
jokes.punchline: Jerry and Kramer, sent to clean up the car, arrive to find — surprise — they also can't get into it.
jokes.explanation: A solid structural callback — the problem that started the episode re-emerges and now traps the people sent to fix it. Good Seinfeld plotting. The comedy is in the inevitability.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, with you it's always yes. Yes to everything. Someone says 'Let's go to the Bronx,' you say yes. You don't even think about it.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not? Because it's the Bronx!

jokes.text: Kramer breaks into George's car with a coat hanger — 'There we go. This is quite a life I lead here.'
jokes.setup: Kramer casually produces a coat hanger and pops the lock.
jokes.punchline: His self-satisfied 'This is quite a life I lead here' — the understated pride of a man who is always prepared for casual car break-ins.
jokes.explanation: The line is delivered by a man who finds car break-ins a routine part of his adventurous lifestyle. Perfectly Kramer — self-mythologizing the mundane. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What? What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Don't look now, but isn't that Sue Ellen Mischke?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Where?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Over there, by the corner.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, that's her. What's she wearing?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Is that a bra?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It looks like a bra.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She's wearing a bra as a top!

jokes.text: Kramer spots Sue Ellen Mischke walking in just a bra and crashes the car.
jokes.setup: Kramer is driving George's freshly cleaned car back to Yankee Stadium.
jokes.punchline: He's so distracted by Sue Ellen in her bra-top that he crashes the car — directly causing the lawsuit plot and the 'bra as fashion' plot.
jokes.explanation: The crash is the hinge event of the entire episode — the Sue Ellen bra situation, seeded in Elaine's revenge, now causes a car accident. Perfect Seinfeld plot mechanics. The fact that one character's petty revenge cascades into an accident and a lawsuit is the show at its most structurally elegant. Rewatch bonus for seeing how all threads connect.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hello!

jokes.text: Oh my God, Kramer, is that woman just wearing a bra? [Kramer cranes to look, crashes car]
jokes.setup: Jerry spots Sue Ellen Mischke walking in her bra-as-top on the street.
jokes.punchline: Kramer, distracted by the sight, crashes George's car — the accident that drives the lawsuit subplot.
jokes.explanation: This is the convergence point of multiple storylines (the bra, the car) in one moment of physical comedy. The payoff is structurally excellent — Elaine's gift has now caused a car accident. The escalation from passive-aggressive gesture to lawsuit is peak Seinfeld plot engineering. The visual of Kramer rear-ending someone because of a bra is high-impact physical comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I can't believe this. I gave her that blouse as a gift and now she's dead!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Elaine, you can't blame yourself. It's not your fault she wore it in a plane crash.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: But that blouse was so bright and colorful. What if the pilot was distracted? What if he couldn't see the runway because he was looking at her outfit?

jokes.text: Jerry: 'Was it a tall woman in a black blazer?' Kramer: 'Yeah.' Jerry: 'That's Sue Ellen Mischke. That's the bra I gave her. She's wearing it as a top.'
jokes.setup: Kramer describes the woman who distracted him into crashing.
jokes.punchline: Jerry/Elaine instantly identifies her — the crash was caused by Elaine's passive-aggressive birthday gift chain reaction.
jokes.explanation: The convergence moment — Elaine's petty rivalry caused Kramer's crash. This is Seinfeld's signature plotting: seemingly unrelated storylines colliding through absurd causality. The recognition beat lands well.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's like she's declaring war on fabrics. I mean, we've come to an understanding with the bra industry, okay? It stays under, we stay out. But now she's breaking the treaty.

jokes.text: A woman is walking around in broad daylight with nothing but a bra on. She's a menace to society.
jokes.setup: The crash has just been revealed to be Sue Ellen's fault (indirectly).
jokes.punchline: Elaine's hyperbolic verdict — 'she's a menace to society' — for the crime of bralessness-turned-fashion.
jokes.explanation: Elaine's righteous indignation here is escalating nicely. The irony that her own gift created the 'menace' is implicit and funnier for being unstated. Solid character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: This is a travesty! A miscarriage of justice! You have been wronged, my friend. The pain and suffering you have endured is immeasurable. We will take this case all the way to the Supreme Court if we have to!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's just a paper cut.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: A paper cut?! A PAPER CUT?! Do you know what a paper cut can lead to? Infection! Gangrene! Amputation! We're talking about a multi-million dollar settlement here!

jokes.text: Kramer: 'Hey, you know, my arm really hurts. I wonder if it's gonna affect my golf swing.'
jokes.setup: Kramer has just been in a car accident and is discussing his injury.
jokes.punchline: His primary concern about the injury is its impact on his amateur golf game — not his health, not the accident.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Kramer — his hierarchy of concerns is completely inverted. The non-sequitur pivot from accident to golf swing is a great character beat and sets up the lawsuit plot. The obliviousness is beautifully specific.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: This outfit is a travesty, a mockery, a farce! It's a travesty, a mockery, a farce of a mockery of a travesty!

jokes.text: I got it! Let's sue her.
jokes.setup: Jerry is skeptical about whether Kramer's shoulder injury is serious. Kramer suddenly has an idea.
jokes.punchline: 'Let's sue her' — Kramer's grand legal epiphany delivered as if it's a stroke of genius.
jokes.explanation: The 'I got it!' setup mimics the sound of genuine inspiration before pivoting to naked greed. Kramer's entrepreneurial instinct — finding an angle in every situation — is perfectly expressed. Functional setup for the Jackie Chiles plot.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: Oh Henry! Oh Henry! Oh Henry! Do you know what this means? This is a very significant confection. The nougat-to-caramel ratio is impeccable. I've been eating these bars for thirty years and I never knew they were made by royalty!

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: We need to get you the best representation. This is a major candy situation. I'm talking serious chocolate bar jurisdiction here.

jokes.text: What do you think, Stan? / Let's go for the green.
jokes.setup: Kramer is in a serious meeting with a lawyer, debating whether to pursue a lawsuit.
jokes.punchline: He consults Stan the Caddy, who answers in golf metaphors — and this is apparently normal.
jokes.explanation: The running gag of Stan as Kramer's life oracle is escalated perfectly — from wardrobe advice to legal strategy. The golf metaphor ('go for the green') applied to a lawsuit is genuinely funny and fits Stan's character.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: We have no case. You can't get blood out of a turnip.

jokes.text: Jackie Chiles: 'She was flouting. That's totally inappropriate. It's lewd, lascivious, salacious, outrageous.'
jokes.setup: Kramer describes the woman in the bra. Jackie immediately begins framing it as a legal case.
jokes.punchline: Jackie's torrent of adjectives — 'lewd, lascivious, salacious, outrageous' — delivered with total professional authority.
jokes.explanation: Jackie Chiles is a brilliantly specific character — a parody of flamboyant attorneys — and his vocabulary cascades are his signature. 'Lewd, lascivious, salacious, outrageous' is perfectly constructed: four adjectives that escalate in moral intensity while describing someone simply not wearing enough clothing. The rhythm and absurd gravity make it one of the most quotable lines in this episode. Justifies 8+ craft: only Jackie can deliver this, the structure is excellent, and it's become culturally iconic.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Don't worry about it. I got it all figured out. I'll just tell them the car got stolen. Insurance will cover it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm serious. I've thought this through. I'll file a police report, say I parked it on the street, came back and it was gone.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? It's foolproof. They're not gonna check anything. They just pay out.

jokes.text: Jackie: 'And she's the heir to the Oh Henry! candy-bar fortune.' / 'Oh Henry!?' / Jackie elaborates on the candy bar's qualities: 'It's got chocolate, peanuts, nougat. It's delicious, scrumptious, outstanding.'
jokes.setup: Kramer mentions Sue Ellen's family fortune. Jackie asks him to repeat it.
jokes.punchline: Jackie starts describing the Oh Henry! bar's ingredients with the same cascading adjective style he uses for legal arguments — suddenly pivoting from law to candy criticism.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic peak for Jackie. His verbal style — cascading descriptors with escalating moral/emotional weight — is applied with perfect absurdity to a candy bar. 'Delicious, scrumptious, outstanding' mirrors 'lewd, lascivious, salacious' exactly in rhythm and structure, but the subject matter couldn't be more different. The character integration is 9.5 because this joke ONLY works with Jackie Chiles — it requires both his verbal tic and his immediate pivot to personal interest. Culturally iconic. Justifies 8.5 craft.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: This car, it has been through something terrible. The blood on the seats, the twisted metal, the shattered glass - it tells a story of violence and suffering.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wilhelm, that's just ketchup and a fender bender.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: A fender bender? No, no. This is a crime scene. A struggle between man and machine. Perhaps a wild animal was involved.

jokes.text: Jackie: 'Like taking candy from a baby.'
jokes.setup: Kramer asks Jackie if they have a case. Jackie assesses it.
jokes.punchline: Jackie uses the idiom 'like taking candy from a baby' — which, given that they're suing the heiress to a candy fortune, is an accidentally perfect pun.
jokes.explanation: The pun works on two levels — the cliché meaning and the literal candy connection. Whether intentional or not within Jackie's character, it lands as a perfectly constructed wordplay moment. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Where's Costanza? I want him found immediately. I don't care if we have to drag the Hudson River, I want results!

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Sir, he's probably just running late...

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Running late? RUNNING LATE?! This is a code red situation! Get me the National Guard, the Coast Guard, and somebody call the Russians — they know how to find people!

jokes.text: George pitches a Yankees trade: Jim Leyritz and Bernie Williams for Barry Bonds — 'Then I have Griffey and Bonds in the same outfield. Now you got a team.'
jokes.setup: George is on the run from his workplace, hiding at a cabin, but can't stop playing assistant GM.
jokes.punchline: George's fantasy baseball move delivered with total confidence — the same confidence that got him a promotion he didn't earn.
jokes.explanation: Good character moment — George living out his Yankees fantasy while on the lam. Funnier for fans who know this is exactly the kind of thing George would obsess over. Period-specific baseball references add authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: Wilhelm
dialogue.line: We can't find George anywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: He's dead. George is dead.

jokes.text: Steinbrenner sees the bashed car and blood: 'Obviously he was in some sort of a terrible car accident, and trouper that he is, he tried to make it into work, sir.'
jokes.setup: Wilhelm reports that George's car is bashed in with blood and George is missing.
jokes.punchline: Wilhelm's spin — rather than assuming something sinister, he frames it as George being a heroic trouper who crawled to work despite injury.
jokes.explanation: The irony is layered — George is actually on a road trip vacation, but his reputation (built entirely on his car being in the lot) is so sterling that the only explanation anyone can imagine for his absence is heroic self-sacrifice. Wilhelm as yes-man spinster adds another layer.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: George is gone. A good man. A good employee. I will miss him.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Now, who's going to take over his desk?

jokes.text: Steinbrenner: 'He's dead. Costanza's dead.' / 'As quickly as he came, he's gone. The poor little guy.' / 'Easy, Big Stein, get it together.'
jokes.setup: Wilhelm reports they can't find George anywhere. Steinbrenner takes the logical leap.
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner immediately declares George dead and has to calm himself down with 'Easy, Big Stein, get it together' — talking to himself in the third person.
jokes.explanation: Steinbrenner talking to himself in the third person — 'Easy, Big Stein' — is a brilliant character beat. The theatrical grief ('as quickly as he came, he's gone') over an employee he barely knows is perfectly absurd. The third-person self-soothing is a genuinely funny comedic device that's character-specific and quotable. Justifies near-8 craft.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're the one who started this whole thing!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Me? You're just trying to cover your tracks!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I only care about what's fair!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Fair? You care about the money!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, you care about being right!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: At least I'm honest about it!

jokes.text: Steinbrenner: 'And line up some candidates to fill that assistant position. We can't grieve forever. Gotta get back to business. Back to business, Wilhelm.'
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner is dramatically mourning George being 'dead.'
jokes.punchline: He instantly pivots from grief to filling George's job — 'We can't grieve forever' — delivered without irony.
jokes.explanation: The cold pivot from theatrical grief to ruthless pragmatism — 'We can't grieve forever. Gotta get back to business.' — is a perfect dark comedy beat. The Steinbrenner character's unsentimental efficiency makes it funnier. Highly quotable outside context.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Frank, I have some terrible news. Your son, George, he's dead.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What?! Dead?!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: How could you let this happen?! He was my boy! My only son!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: And another thing - those trades you made last season were a disaster! Trading away Rodriguez for nothing?! What were you thinking?!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: You destroy my son's life with your incompetence, and you destroy the team too!

jokes.text: Kramer confronts Jerry after finding Sue Ellen in his apartment: 'Little Miss Candy Bar paid a visit, didn't she?'
jokes.setup: Jerry has been reluctant to testify against Sue Ellen, and now Kramer has found out why.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's film-noir framing of Sue Ellen as a femme fatale — 'Little Miss Candy Bar' — delivered with theatrical accusation.
jokes.explanation: 'Little Miss Candy Bar' is a perfect Kramer coinage — it combines the candy fortune reference with a diminutive that implies both contempt and enchantment. His noir-detective framing of a soap opera situation is character-specific and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Should I tell my parents I'm alive?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, they might be happier thinking you're dead.

jokes.text: Kramer accuses Jerry: 'She's twisted you around her little finger and now you're willing to sell me and Elaine and whoever else you have to right down the river.' Jerry: 'And what about you? Trying to bilk an innocent bystander out of a family fortune built on sweat and toil manufacturing quality Oh Henry! candy bars for honest, hardworking Americans.'
jokes.setup: Kramer and Jerry are in a moral standoff — Kramer accuses Jerry of being seduced, Jerry accuses Kramer of being greedy.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's solemn defense of 'a family fortune built on sweat and toil manufacturing quality Oh Henry! candy bars for honest, hardworking Americans' — the candy bar elevated to sacred American institution.
jokes.explanation: Jerry's earnest invocation of working-class candy-bar manufacturing values to defend a rich heiress he has a crush on is beautifully absurd. The rhetorical escalation of both arguments mirrors the episode's general logic of petty disputes becoming grandiose moral battles.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Mr. Steinbrenner, I can explain my absence. I was in the Amazon rainforest studying the mating habits of the jaguar. My research team and I were attacked by a swarm of bullet ants. I had to fashion a makeshift tourniquet from vines and navigate back to civilization using only the stars and my knowledge of osmosis.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Costanza, that's the most ridiculous thing I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's all true, sir. Every word.

jokes.text: 'You're just out for sex!' / 'You're just out for money!' — the two men stare at each other, both correct.
jokes.setup: Kramer and Jerry have been making elaborate moral arguments at each other.
jokes.punchline: The arguments collapse into pure, naked honesty — each perfectly and accurately accusing the other — followed by a beat of mutual recognition.
jokes.explanation: Perfect structural deflation — all the elaborate rhetoric collapses into two blunt truths. The symmetry is beautifully constructed, each man exactly right about the other in five words. The reaction beat after the accusations (implied silent beat as they both absorb being called out correctly) makes it land even harder. Justifies 8+ craft: excellent structure, economy near-perfect, character-specific, and earned through the buildup.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I could survive on grubs.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Grubs? What kind of grubs?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, grubs. Insects.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: And they're... edible?

jokes.text: Steinbrenner personally shows up at the Costanza house to deliver news of George's 'death.'
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner decided to personally notify George's parents that he's dead.
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of the Yankees owner making a house call to inform parents their son — who is alive on a road trip — has died.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'George is missing' to 'I'll personally go tell his parents he's dead' is perfectly Steinbrenner — theatrical, disproportionate, and completely sincere. The set-up for the Jay Buhner joke.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Chaos doesn't work for the Yankees. We need order, we need discipline, we need structure. That's what wins championships. That's what I've built here. And I'm not going to let some fly-by-night operation come in here and destroy everything I've worked for.

jokes.text: Mrs. Costanza: 'I can't believe it. He was so young. How could this have happened?' Steinbrenner: 'He'd been logging heavy hours. First one in, last one to leave. The kid was a human dynamo.'
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner eulogizes George to his grieving mother.
jokes.punchline: He describes a George who is the complete opposite of the real George — dedicated, tireless, a 'human dynamo' — and the mother's reaction is not grief but confusion.
jokes.explanation: The gap between the George Steinbrenner knows (a phantom of dedication) and the George his parents know (a lazy schemer) is the central irony. 'A human dynamo' applied to George Costanza is perfectly absurd. Mrs. Costanza's response sets up the next joke.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Mr. Seinfeld, you were present at the incident in question?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes, Your Honor.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: And you saw the defendant, Sue Ellen Mischke?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I did. She was... she looked really good that day.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Mr. Seinfeld, please stick to the facts.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, she had this dress on, and her hair was perfect. I mean, if you're going to accuse someone, at least they should look like that.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: This is a court of law, not a dating service.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm just being honest. Whatever she did, I'm willing to overlook it.

jokes.text: Estelle: 'Are you sure you're talking about George?' — beat — Steinbrenner: 'You are Mr. and Mrs. Costanza?'
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner has just described George as a human dynamo who was first in and last to leave.
jokes.punchline: George's own mother's disbelief is so total that Steinbrenner has to verify he's at the right house.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The setup is perfect: the most positive possible eulogy for George, delivered to his mother, who is so familiar with his actual character that she can't accept it. Her doubt is so complete that Steinbrenner genuinely wonders if he has the right family. The economy is superb — nine words from Estelle demolish George's entire invented persona. Justifies 8.5+ craft: exceptional structure, maximum economy, works only with these specific characters, completely earned.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie
dialogue.line: Stan, I need legal advice. What should I do?

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: Well, the key is to keep your head down, stay focused, and remember: it's all about the follow-through.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie
dialogue.line: That's your legal strategy?

dialogue.speaker: Stan
dialogue.line: Works on the golf course. Why shouldn't it work in court?

jokes.text: Frank Costanza explodes at Steinbrenner: 'What the hell did you trade Jay Buhner for? He had 30 home runs and over 100 RBIs last year. He's got a rocket for an arm. You don't know what the hell you're doing!'
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner has just told Frank that George is dead. Frank's first response is not grief.
jokes.punchline: Frank completely ignores the death announcement and launches into a furious critique of the Jay Buhner trade — revealing his baseball priorities supersede his parental instincts.
jokes.explanation: The episode's comedic zenith. Frank's complete non-reaction to his son's reported death — bypassed entirely in favor of a furious baseball trade argument — is the Seinfeld character comedy ethos at its absolute peak. It simultaneously reveals Frank's character perfectly (baseball above family), satirizes the Steinbrenner Yankees, and creates maximum comedic surprise. The specificity — 30 home runs, 100 RBIs, 'rocket for an arm' — makes it feel like a speech Frank has been waiting years to deliver. 'You don't know what the hell you're doing!' is iconically quotable. This is easily the best sitcom joke in the episode and rivals the anchor examples. Justifies 8.9 craft.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: This is unconscionable! You took the advice of a caddy? A CADDY?! Do you know what a caddy is? A caddy is a guy who carries clubs! I have gone to school for eight years to get my law degree, and you're taking tips from a man who makes his living hauling sticks!

jokes.text: Frank Costanza's answering machine message, played while Steinbrenner is still in the room: 'I'm not here. Leave a message.' Then: 'Jerry, it's Frank Costanza. Mr. Steinbrenner's here. George is dead. Call me back.'
jokes.setup: Frank has apparently called Jerry to relay the death news while Steinbrenner visits.
jokes.punchline: The message is delivered in Frank's signature flat, matter-of-fact tone — 'George is dead. Call me back.' — as if it's a minor logistical update.
jokes.explanation: Frank's voice message delivery is perfect — the total absence of parental anguish in favor of a terse update is a brilliant character joke. 'George is dead. Call me back.' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode. The economy (four words for the death news) is superb. Justifies 8+ craft.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: Your Honor, the demonstration was fundamentally flawed. You cannot simply take a bra off a woman and expect it to fit properly on another woman. Every woman is different! The architecture is different, the support system is different.

dialogue.speaker: Jackie Chiles
dialogue.line: This is not unlike the glove situation. If it doesn't fit, you must acquit!

jokes.text: George: 'I gotta head back right away.' Jerry: 'You gotta call your parents.' George: 'I can't. Steinbrenner might be there.' Jerry: 'Aren't you gonna tell your parents you're still alive?' George: 'They could use the break.'
jokes.setup: George has learned his parents think he's dead. Jerry urges him to call them immediately.
jokes.punchline: 'They could use the break.' — George calmly decides to let his parents believe he's dead because they might enjoy the respite.
jokes.explanation: The single best joke in the episode and one of the great Seinfeld lines. 'They could use the break' — four words — encapsulates the entire George-parent relationship, his dark nihilism, his self-awareness, and his complete moral indifference. It's a devastating character insight delivered as a throwaway. Every element works: the setup (Jerry's earnest concern), the structure (perfectly timed beat before the punchline), the character integration (only George), the economy (four words), and it's completely earned through seasons of Costanza family dysfunction. Rivals the anchor examples. Justifies 9.0 craft.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's a great outfit. Really subtle. I'm sure nobody will notice you in that.

jokes.text: Kramer wanders into Jerry's apartment holding a bra, rhapsodizing about 'what exquisite beauty,' and explaining he ran down the hall but 'the elevator door closed. It was not to be. Perhaps our paths will cross again someday.'
jokes.setup: Kramer appears in the apartment holding a bra, completely transported by what he's seen.
jokes.punchline: His romantic, fatalistic musing about a woman in a bra he briefly saw in the hallway — treating it like a doomed love story.
jokes.explanation: Kramer's theatrical romanticism is perfectly deployed here — he treats a glimpse of a woman in a bra like a scene from a French film. 'It was not to be' is particularly funny. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Kramer declares he wants to market the bra 'as a new direction in women's fashion' — referencing 'Zelda Fitzgerald, somebody of the '20s, wearing this at wild parties, driving all the men crazy.'
jokes.setup: Kramer has been inspired by Sue Ellen's bra-as-top and now wants to commercialize it.
jokes.punchline: His fashion pitch invokes Zelda Fitzgerald as 'somebody of the '20s' — a halfhearted historical reference that betrays he doesn't quite know who Zelda Fitzgerald is.
jokes.explanation: 'Zelda Fitzgerald, somebody of the '20s' is a wonderful Kramer-ism — he's name-dropping with just enough specificity to sound knowledgeable while revealing he only has the vaguest idea who she was. His entrepreneurial schemes always have this half-baked grandiosity.

jokes.text: George's elaborate lie to Steinbrenner: 'After the car accident, I crawled into a ditch. I managed to survive on grubs and puddle water until a kindly old gentleman picked me up.'
jokes.setup: George has to explain where he's been since the car was found damaged with blood.
jokes.punchline: His survival story — grubs, puddle water, and a kindly old gentleman — is maximally dramatic for someone who was at a cabin in the woods.
jokes.explanation: George's commitment to his lie is total — the 'grubs and puddle water' survival narrative is perfectly over-the-top. The specificity ('puddle water,' 'kindly old gentleman') is what makes it funny. Pure George Costanza.

jokes.text: Steinbrenner: 'Grubs, huh? Gotta admit, I never tasted one of those.'
jokes.setup: George has just described eating grubs to survive in a ditch.
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner's only response to this dramatic survival story is to muse about never having tried grubs.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Steinbrenner moment — his total, genuine engagement with the most irrelevant detail in George's dramatic story. The deadpan curiosity about grubs is wonderfully absurd and character-specific.

jokes.text: Grubs, huh? Gotta admit, I never tasted one of those.
jokes.setup: George claims he survived on grubs and puddle water.
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner's only response to this insane story is a thoughtful, almost wistful admission that he's never eaten a grub — completely bypassing any skepticism.
jokes.explanation: A perfect Steinbrenner moment. His response is so completely beside the point — no skepticism, no outrage, just genuine curiosity about grubs — that it reveals him as a magnificent buffoon. The character integration is exceptional. 'Gotta admit, I never tasted one of those' is a great specific line.

jokes.text: George didn't get the promotion because he was 'dead.' Steinbrenner: 'You were dead. We couldn't sit on our hands. We had to make a move.'
jokes.setup: George survived his 'death' and returned expecting the promotion he'd been promised.
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner's pragmatic explanation — George was dead, they needed to fill the position, business is business.
jokes.explanation: The cosmic justice of George losing his promotion because he was presumed dead from a vacation he took to help his chances is perfect Seinfeld irony. 'We couldn't sit on our hands' is a great line — all business logic applied to a false death.

jokes.text: Steinbrenner: 'And I can tell you this; Chaos does not work for the New York Yankees. Not as long as I'm running the show!'
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner is explaining that he can't give George special treatment for surviving his 'death.'
jokes.punchline: His passionate declaration that 'Chaos does not work for the New York Yankees' — delivered by a character who is himself a monument to organizational chaos.
jokes.explanation: The irony is built into the character — the historical Steinbrenner was famous for being erratic and chaotic, yet this version declares himself an enemy of chaos. Good ironic character comedy.

jokes.text: In court, Kramer is asked his last golf score before the injury. 'Three under par.' Jackie: 'That's what the professionals shoot, isn't it?' Kramer: 'Well, if they're lucky.'
jokes.setup: Jackie is establishing Kramer's golf prowess for the jury to show what he lost.
jokes.punchline: Kramer casually claiming he shoots scores that professionals 'shoot if they're lucky' — delivered without a trace of self-awareness.
jokes.explanation: Kramer's delusion about his golf game is perfectly calibrated — 'well, if they're lucky' is exactly how he would view his amateur scores against professional benchmarks. The courtroom setting makes the absurdity sharper.

jokes.text: Jerry on the witness stand: 'I don't remember.' / 'Maybe.' / Then breaks: 'All right, I saw her. I saw her. And she was beautiful in that bra. I'm crazy about her. I love her whole free-swinging, freewheeling attitude.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is under oath and clearly trying to protect Sue Ellen by not testifying. Jackie presses him.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's resistance crumbles not into neutral testimony but into a passionate declaration of love — in open court.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'I don't remember' to 'I love her whole free-swinging, freewheeling attitude' is perfectly constructed. The courtroom collapse — perjury gives way to involuntary romantic declaration — is a satisfying payoff for the Jerry/Sue Ellen subplot. 'Free-swinging, freewheeling' is a wonderful phrase. Good structure.

jokes.text: All right, I saw her. I saw her. And she was beautiful in that bra. I'm crazy about her. I love her whole free-swinging, freewheeling attitude.
jokes.setup: Forced under oath to admit he saw Sue Ellen, Jerry breaks entirely.
jokes.punchline: Jerry doesn't just admit to seeing her — he launches into a full romantic testimonial in open court, confessing his infatuation while Kramer watches his lawsuit collapse.
jokes.explanation: A great escalation moment. Jerry going from 'I don't remember' to 'I love her whole free-swinging, freewheeling attitude' in a courtroom is a perfect comedic arc. 'Free-swinging, freewheeling attitude' is a deliberately absurd phrase that also functions as an inadvertent bra pun. High impact.

jokes.text: Kramer asks Stan for legal strategy during the trial. Stan advises: 'You're close. You're on the green. You just have to go for the cup.' His advice: 'Have her try on the bra.'
jokes.setup: Mid-trial, Kramer needs strategic advice. He turns to Stan the Caddy.
jokes.punchline: Stan gives golf metaphor advice that somehow translates into sound-but-catastrophic legal strategy — 'have her try on the bra' — and Jackie is furious that Kramer takes it.
jokes.explanation: The Stan running gag reaches its peak absurdity — a caddy is being consulted during a trial, overriding an experienced attorney. The golf metaphors applied to courtroom strategy ('you're on the green') and the resulting disaster is a perfect payoff for the running gag. Jackie's outrage is warranted and funny.

jokes.text: Jackie, to the court: 'We request at this time that Miss Mischke try on the bra.'
jokes.setup: Following Stan's advice, Jackie makes his request to the court.
jokes.punchline: A formal, solemn legal request to have a defendant try on a bra in open court — presented with full professional gravity.
jokes.explanation: The formal legal language applied to the request makes it funnier — Jackie says it with the gravity of a seasoned attorney making a pivotal procedural move. The absurdity of the courtroom bra-fitting is perfect Seinfeld.

jokes.text: Sue Ellen tries on the bra in court. It doesn't fit.
jokes.setup: The entire case hinges on proving the bra was worn publicly. Jackie and Kramer are confident.
jokes.punchline: The bra doesn't fit — Stan's brilliant strategy has destroyed the case.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is perfectly constructed — Stan's advice seemed logical (if she wore it, it'll fit) but the execution collapses because of a leotard. The visual of the bra not fitting in a courtroom is inherently absurd and a great physical comedy moment. Strong structure.

jokes.text: Jackie: 'You damn fools! Look at that. We got nothing now. Nothing. I've been practicing law for 25 years, you're listening to a caddy.'
jokes.setup: The bra doesn't fit and the case is lost.
jokes.punchline: Jackie's furious, devastated breakdown — 'you're listening to a caddy' — the perfect summation of the absurdity.
jokes.explanation: 'I've been practicing law for 25 years, you're listening to a caddy' is an almost perfect comedic line — it delivers on the entire episode's Stan running gag while being independently hilarious. Jackie's theatrical devastation combined with the objectively correct diagnosis of what went wrong makes this one of the episode's most memorable moments. Justifies 8+ craft: excellent structure, peak character integration for Jackie, highly quotable, completely earned through episode-long setup.

jokes.text: Jackie: 'You can't let the defendant control the key piece of evidence. Plus, she's trying it on over a leotard. Of course it won't fit over a leotard. A bra's gotta fit right up against a person's skin. Like a glove!'
jokes.setup: Jackie is furiously dissecting why the bra gambit failed.
jokes.punchline: 'A bra's gotta fit right up against a person's skin. Like a glove!' — Jackie channeling his inner Johnnie Cochran for lingerie logistics.
jokes.explanation: The most culturally resonant joke in the episode and one of the most famous Seinfeld moments. 'A bra's gotta fit right up against a person's skin. Like a glove!' is a direct parody of Johnnie Cochran's 'if it doesn't fit, you must acquit' — applied to lingerie. The structure mirrors the O.J. Simpson trial moment perfectly, Jackie IS that kind of attorney, and the specificity ('right up against a person's skin') before the iconic 'like a glove' makes it land perfectly. This is a 9+ craft joke: exceptional originality, perfect structure, only possible with Jackie Chiles, economical, and completely earned. It has become a cultural touchstone.

jokes.text: She's trying it on over a leotard. Of course it won't fit over a leotard. A bra's gotta fit right up against a person's skin. Like a glove!
jokes.setup: Jackie is furiously post-mortem-ing why the bra didn't fit.
jokes.punchline: 'A bra's gotta fit right up against a person's skin. Like a glove!' — Jackie inadvertently invokes the O.J. glove defense he's been parodying, while trying to explain why he lost. The meta-irony is spectacular.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single greatest joke. Jackie Chiles — who has been a parody of Johnnie Cochran throughout — says 'like a glove!' while explaining why the bra-glove strategy failed. It's simultaneously the punchline to the O.J. parody, a callback to 'if it doesn't fit you must acquit,' and a perfect character moment (Jackie is SO outraged he's accidentally quoting the famous defense). The structure is flawless: the setup took the entire episode, the punchline lands in three words. This is genuinely exceptional television comedy writing — comparable to the best Seinfeld moments. Closest thing in this episode to a 9.5.

tmdb_overview: Kramer sues Elaine's friend for sinking his golf career; George's apparent work ethic impresses his bosses.
insight: Caddy subplot escalates absurdly while character comedy sustains 1.8 jokes per minute.
